1: שְׁמִינִית (shĕmînît, "Sheminith") — literally "eighth"; a musical term appearing also in Ps 12:1. Its precise meaning is uncertain — possibly an eight-stringed instrument, a particular musical mode, or a pitch register (Sheminith may indicate a low-pitched performance, contrasted with ʿalāmôt in Ps 46:1). אַל-בְּאַפְּךָ תוֹכִיחֵנִי (ʾal-bĕʾappĕkā tôkîḥênî, "do not rebuke me in your anger") — yākhaḥ (hiphil tôkîaḥ) is a legal term: to argue a case, to convict, to bring a charge. The Psalmist is not asking God to be kind instead of strict, but to not prosecute him in anger. The same plea appears in Ps 38:1. The fear is not of discipline per se but of the full force of divine judicial wrath.
2: חָנֵּנִי יְהוָה כִּי אֻמְלַל אָנִי (ḥonnênî YHWH kî ʾumlal ʾānî, "Be gracious to me, O LORD, for I am weak") — ḥānan: to be gracious, to show mercy, to give something not deserved. ʾUmlal (pual of ʾāmal): weak, languishing, withered — a passive form suggesting a state being done to him, not chosen. עֲצָמַי נִבְהָלוּ (ʿăṣāmay nibhālû, "my bones are in agony") — nābhal (niphal): to be dismayed, to be terrified — bones as the deepest physical structure of the person are shaken to the core. Ancient medicine located the deepest vitality in the bones.
3: נַפְשִׁי נִבְהֲלָה מְאֹד (napshî nibhălāh mĕʾōd, "my whole being is deeply troubled") — nefesh (soul/life/whole self — often the entire person) is deeply dismayed (mĕʾōd: greatly, intensely). וְאַתָּה יְהוָה עַד-מָתָי (wĕʾattāh YHWH ʿad-mātay, "But you, O LORD — how long?") — the question breaks off: the sentence is not finished. "How long?" without a verb — the most compressed form of lament, the half-spoken cry. The Hebrew allows the incompleteness to stand as the most natural expression of extremity.
4: שׁוּבָה יְהוָה חַלְּצָה נַפְשִׁי (shûbāh YHWH ḥallĕṣāh napshî, "Turn back, O LORD, rescue my life") — shûb (return/turn) is used of God turning his face or attention toward the suppliant. Ḥālaṣ (piel): to strip off, to deliver — the image is of being stripped free from a tight place (related to the root in v5:1 "tight/distress"). לְמַעַן חַסְדֶּךָ (lĕmaʿan ḥasdekā, "for the sake of your steadfast love") — the appeal to ḥesed as the motive for rescue: God's covenant loyalty demands he act on behalf of his covenant partner.
5: כִּי אֵין בַּמָּוֶת זִכְרֶךָ (kî ʾên bammāwet zikrekkā, "for among the dead no one remembers you") — a theologically significant statement of pre-resurrection anthropology: in Sheol (בִּשְׁאוֹל), the dead cannot give thanks or praise. The Psalmist uses the logic of God's interest in ongoing worship to argue for rescue: if he dies, God loses a worshiper. This is not a bargaining chip but a recognition that praise belongs to life, not death — a reality the resurrection overturns (see the connections in 1 Cor 15:20).
6: יָגַעְתִּי בְאַנְחָתִי (yāgaʿtî bĕʾanḥātî, "I am exhausted with my moaning") — yāgaʿ: weary, fatigued; the moaning itself is exhausting. אַשְׂחֶה בְכָל-לַיְלָה מִטָּתִי (ʾaśḥeh bĕkol-layĕlāh miṭṭātî, "every night I drench my bed") — śāḥāh: to float, to swim; the bed is "flooded" with tears. The hyperbole communicates total saturation — no part of sleep unmarked by weeping.
7: עָשְׁשָׁה מִכַּעַס עֵינִי (ʿāshĕshāh mikkacʿas ʿênî, "My eye has grown dim from grief") — ʿāshash (poel): to decay, to waste away — the eyes literally deteriorating from prolonged weeping and grief. Kaʿas (grief, vexation, anger): the word can cover both sorrow and the anger of frustration. The physical deterioration (eyes wasting) is a sign of the total impact of the crisis on the body.
8: סוּרוּ מִמֶּנִּי כָּל-פֹּעֲלֵי אָוֶן (sûrû mimmennî kol-pōʿălê ʾāwen, "Depart from me, all you who do evil") — the sudden turn from weeping (v1-7) to command (v8-10). The Psalmist now dismisses the wicked as those whose presence is already rendered impossible by the divine response. The confidence of v8 is grounded in v8b: שָׁמַע יְהוָה קוֹל בִּכְיִי (shāmaʿ YHWH qôl bikeyî) — "the LORD has heard the sound of my weeping." The weeping was not private; it was heard.
9: שָׁמַע יְהוָה תְּחִנָּתִי (shāmaʿ YHWH tĕḥinnātî, "The LORD has heard my supplication") — tĕḥinnāh: a plea for grace/mercy, the verbal noun from ḥānan (be gracious). The LORD has heard and now receives (יִקַּח, yiqqaḥ, imperfect of certainty): the prayer is accepted. The shift from petition (v1-7) to announcement (v8-10) is abrupt — the text does not explain what changed, only that it did. The Psalmist perceives the answer and declares it.
10: יֵבֹשׁוּ וְיִבָּהֲלוּ מְאֹד (yêbōshû wĕyibbāhălû mĕʾōd, "shall be ashamed and greatly disturbed") — bûsh (to be ashamed, to be disappointed in one's expectations) and bāhal (to be terrified, dismayed — the same word used of the Psalmist's bones in v2). The enemies will experience what the Psalmist experienced — reversal. יָשֻׁבוּ יֵבֹשׁוּ רָגַע (yāshûbû yêbōshû rāgaʿ, "they shall turn back and be disgraced in an instant") — rāgaʿ: suddenly, in a moment — the swift judgment of the confident Psalmist who has heard God's answer.
